sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Pleural mesothelioma is a rare malignancy arising from the serosal lining of the pleura, accounting for approximately 85% of all mesotheliomas. It typically affects individuals over 60 years of age with a history of asbestos exposure, often with a latency period of more than 32 years [MS-2]. Patients commonly present with dyspnea and chest wall pain, which are often the most frequent symptoms [MS-3]. Additional symptoms include pleural effusion, fatigue, insomnia, cough, chest wall mass, loss of appetite, and weight loss [MS-3]. Most patients are diagnosed without distant metastases, and central nervous system involvement is rare [MS-3]. The clinical presentation can be insidious, leading to delayed diagnosis due to non-specific symptoms [PM-A]. On physical examination, signs may include decreased breath sounds, dullness to percussion, and palpable chest wall masses. Imaging typically reveals pleural thickening and effusion on chest CT with contrast [PM-1]. The diagnosis requires a multidisciplinary approach, including pathologic confirmation via biopsy, as cytology alone is often insufficient [MS-3].

symptoms.name: Dyspnea
symptoms.detail: Shortness of breath, often progressive and due to pleural effusion or tumor burden. It is a cardinal symptom that may worsen with activity or at rest.

symptoms.name: Chest wall pain
symptoms.detail: Pain may be localized or diffuse, often related to tumor invasion of chest wall structures or pleural irritation. It can be dull or sharp and may radiate.

symptoms.name: Pleural effusion
symptoms.detail: Accumulation of fluid in the pleural space, causing respiratory symptoms such as dyspnea and cough. Effusions are often exudative and may be recurrent.

symptoms.name: Fatigue
symptoms.detail: Generalized tiredness, may be related to cancer cachexia, anemia, or systemic inflammation.

symptoms.name: Insomnia
symptoms.detail: Difficulty sleeping, potentially due to pain, dyspnea, or anxiety related to the disease.

symptoms.name: Cough
symptoms.detail: May be dry or productive, related to pleural irritation, compression of airways, or infection.

symptoms.name: Chest wall mass
symptoms.detail: Palpable mass in advanced cases, indicating local invasion of the chest wall or soft tissues.

symptoms.name: Loss of appetite
symptoms.detail: Anorexia, contributing to weight loss and cachexia.

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unexplained weight loss, often significant, defined as >5% loss over 6 months. It is a poor prognostic indicator.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Persistent or worsening dyspnea in a patient with asbestos exposure should raise suspicion and prompt immediate evaluation.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Unexplained chest pain, especially if persistent or associated with other symptoms, warrants investigation for malignancy.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Recurrent or large pleural effusion in the context of asbestos exposure is concerning and should be evaluated with cytology and biopsy.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Unexplained fatigue with other constitutional symptoms should prompt a comprehensive cancer workup.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Insomnia in the context of other symptoms may indicate advanced disease or poor performance status.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Persistent cough without clear cause, especially with other red flags, should be evaluated.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Any palpable mass in the chest wall in a patient with risk factors is alarming and requires urgent biopsy.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Unintentional appetite loss is a red flag for malignancy and should be assessed.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Weight loss is a classic alarm symptom for cancer and necessitates immediate investigation.
